category_title: Students with communication difficulties and reactive behavioral outbursts when challenged
minimal_context: Angus is sitting in a circle with his group discussing the story Laura and Paul.
dialogue:
TUTOR: We are going to discuss the story Laura and Paul today in our circle. Angus, what do you think about what Paul did in the story?

STUDENT: I think... um... I think Paul was right. He did the right thing.

TUTOR: Another student in the circle just said they disagree with your view about Paul. What do you say to that?

STUDENT: (Gets flustered) But... no! I said he was right! Why are they saying that? They are wrong!

TUTOR: They just have a different view. Keep trying to explain your side.

STUDENT: (Makes an angry face at the other student and kicks the chair) Shut up! You're stupid! I'm not talking anymore!

options.A: Explore and define the student's feelings about the story through repetition and expansion during the group discussion.
options.B: Force a change in perspective and ask the student if the situation would be fair if he were Paul.
options.C: Enforce that participants cannot use technical language or jargon, requiring any word that may act as a barrier to understanding to be explained.
options.D: Review the story with the group and question the student again about the critical elements of the text.
